metadata.title: Introduction to Estate Planning
metadata.subtitle: Protecting Yourself and Those You Care About Through Wills, Trusts, and Essential Legal Documents

content.title: Introduction to Estate Planning
content.subtitle: Protecting Yourself and Those You Care About in {{STATE_NAME}}

content.label: When 24 Becomes Too Late
content.question:
Sarah (24) was critically injured in a car accident...

Without healthcare directive: Estranged parents made medical decisions against her known wishes
Without power of attorney: Partner couldn't access bank accounts to pay rent
Cost to prevent this: Less than $300

Today's Question: What documents protect you when you can't speak for yourself?

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION PROMPTS (5 min):
• Ask: "Why do you think most young adults don't have these documents?"
• Key insight: Estate planning isn't about death or taxes—it's about protection during incapacity.
• Sarah's story shows real consequences—family conflict, financial chaos, ignored wishes.
• Emphasize: These documents are MORE critical for young adults than seniors (longer potential incapacity period).

objectives.description: essential estate planning documents: wills, trusts, power of attorney, and healthcare directives
the probate process and strategies for avoiding or minimizing it
revocable and irrevocable trusts and understand when each is appropriate
how beneficiary designations interact with wills and why they're critical
informed decisions about basic estate planning needs appropriate for your life stage

layoutData.title: Creating legal documents that answer five critical questions about your life and death
bulletPoints: 1. What happens to your assets when you die?
2. Who makes financial decisions if you can't?
3. Who makes medical decisions if you're incapacitated?
4. Who cares for your children if something happens to you?
5. How can you minimize legal complexity and cost for your family?
layoutData.keyPoint: Estate planning is essential at any age. A 25-year-old needs healthcare directives as much as a 75-year-old—perhaps more, since younger people may face decades of care if seriously injured.

infoCard.title: What Wills DON'T Control
items: These pass by beneficiary designation, NOT your will!

content.headerTitle: Dying Without a Will: Intestate Succession

scenario.name: What Happens in {{STATE_NAME}} Without a Will
paragraphs: When you die without a valid will, state intestacy laws determine who inherits.
• Unmarried partners receive NOTHING—even after decades together
• Assets divided by rigid formulas that rarely match your wishes
• Courts appoint guardians without knowing your preferences
• Entirely public process, expensive, time-consuming (6-24 months)
In {{STATE_NAME}}: {{STATE_NAME}} intestacy laws follow state probate code formulas

outcomes.label: The Cost
outcomes.detail: Probate typically costs 3-7% of estate value ($3,000-$7,000 per $100,000) plus 6-24 months of delays

leftColumn.title: Revocable Living Trust
items: ✅ You control assets during your life
❌ No tax benefits or asset protection

rightColumn.title: Irrevocable Trust
items: Best for: Advanced planning, asset protection, tax concerns

content.headerTitle: Trusts Aren't Just for the Wealthy

scenario.name: Young Parents: Marcus & Sarah (Ages 29/30)
paragraphs: Situation: Two young children (ages 2 & 4), $600K life insurance, modest other assets
Problem: Can't name children directly as life insurance beneficiaries—minors can't own assets
Without Trust: Court appoints custodian until age 18, then $600K lump sum to teenager
• Trustee manages funds for children's benefit

outcomes.label: Key Insight
outcomes.detail: Estate size matters less than family complexity. Young parents with life insurance need trusts more than wealthy retirees with adult children.

leftPanel.title: What Is Financial POA?
paragraphs: Authorizes someone to handle financial matters on your behalf if you're incapacitated
Durable POA: Remains effective during incapacity (what you want)

content.headerTitle: Document 4: Healthcare Power of Attorney & Living Will

leftPanel.title: Healthcare Power of Attorney
paragraphs: Authorizes someone to make medical decisions when you can't
Choose: Someone who knows your values and will honor your wishes

infoCard.title: Living Will (Advance Directive)
items: Prevents family conflict over difficult medical decisions during crisis

content.headerTitle: Document 5: Beneficiary Designations — The Will Override

layoutData.title: CRITICAL: Beneficiary designations TRUMP your will!
bulletPoints: Assets that bypass wills: Retirement accounts (401k, IRA), life insurance, POD bank accounts, TOD investment accounts
Primary beneficiary: First in line to receive assets if alive at your death
Contingent beneficiary: Backup—receives assets only if primary has died before you
ALWAYS name contingent beneficiaries—without them, assets go to your estate (probate) if primary predeceases you
layoutData.keyPoint: For most people, retirement accounts and life insurance represent the majority of wealth—these pass by beneficiary form, NOT will. Review beneficiaries annually and after all major life events (marriage, divorce, births, deaths).

leftColumn.title: Mistake 1: Never Updated After Divorce
items: Michael (42) divorced 8 years ago, remarried 5 years ago
Current wife gets only $250K (home via will)

centerColumn.title: Mistake 2: Minor Children Named Directly
items: Amanda (35) named 8-year-old daughter directly on $250K life insurance
Result: Daughter depleted entire inheritance by age 24
Prevention: Name TRUST as beneficiary, not child directly!

rightColumn.title: Mistake 3: No Contingent Beneficiaries
items: Karen (67) named only her mother (89) as primary

leftPanel.title: Why Guardian Designation Is Critical
paragraphs: • Courts decide who raises your children WITHOUT knowing your preferences
• Children may go to foster care temporarily while relatives battle for custody
• Outcome may not be who you'd choose
• Family relationships damaged by custody battles

infoCard.title: Choosing Guardians
items: • Who shares your values & parenting philosophy?
• Who has energy/family situation for more children?
• Have you discussed this with them?
Consider: Guardian of person (raises children) vs. trustee (manages money) can be different people

layoutData.title: The court-supervised legal process of validating a will, paying debts, and distributing assets
paragraphs: 1. File will with court (if will exists)
Privacy: Entirely public record—anyone can see what you owned and who inherited
layoutData.keyPoint: Strategies to avoid probate: Revocable living trust, beneficiary designations, joint ownership with right of survivorship, POD/TOD accounts

leftColumn.title: Young Singles (18-30, No Dependents)
items: 3. Basic will (if assets > $25K or serious relationship)
Why: Incapacity protection MORE important than death planning at this stage

centerColumn.title: Married/Serious Relationship
items: Why: Unmarried partners have NO automatic rights—will is essential

rightColumn.title: Parents of Minor Children
items: Why: Protecting children is non-negotiable—do this NOW

leftPanel.title: Estate & Inheritance Taxes in {{STATE_NAME}}
paragraphs: Most people won't face estate taxes—but planning still essential for avoiding probate, protecting children, and ensuring wishes honored

infoCard.title: {{STATE_NAME}} Probate & Intestacy
items: • {{STATE_NAME}} probate code determines asset distribution without will

leftColumn.title: Procrastination
items: "I'm too young to need estate planning"
Young adults face DECADES of potential care if seriously injured
Healthcare directive & POA are MORE critical for young adults than seniors

rightColumn.title: Ignoring Beneficiaries
items: Ex-spouses still getting assets years after divorce
Minor children named directly (lump sum at 18)

leftPanel.title: DIY vs. Attorney: When to Use Each

infoCard.title: 30-Day Action Plan
items: Week 1: Identify your priority documents based on life stage
Week 2: Choose agents (healthcare proxy, financial POA, guardians)
Week 3: Create or update documents (attorney, online service, or state forms)
Week 4: Review beneficiaries, store documents securely, inform family of locations

content.question:
1. Life Stage Assessment: Based on your current situation, which estate planning documents are most critical for YOU right now?

2. Barriers: What prevents young adults from creating these documents? How can those barriers be overcome?

3. Conversations: Who would you trust as your healthcare proxy? Financial agent? Guardian (if applicable)? Have you had these conversations with them?

4. Commitment: What specific estate planning action will you take within 30 days?

slides.notes:
DISCUSSION FACILITATION:

1. Life stage: Help students self-identify priority documents. Everyone 18+ needs healthcare directive & POA. Parents need guardians URGENTLY.

2. Barriers: Cost ($100-500 is manageable), perceived complexity (online services simplify), feeling "too young" (incapacity doesn't discriminate), procrastination (create 30-day timeline).

3. Conversations: Emphasize that choosing agents requires difficult but important conversations. Don't assume—ASK if people are willing.

4. Commitment: Encourage specific, time-bound actions: "Create healthcare directive by March 30" not "look into estate planning someday."

takeaways.title: Estate Planning Is for Everyone 18+
takeaways.description: Healthcare directives and POAs protect you while LIVING. More immediately relevant than wills for young adults. Create these first.

takeaways.title: Parents Need Guardians Immediately
takeaways.description: If you have minor children, guardian designation is URGENT—not optional, regardless of age or assets. Do this NOW.

takeaways.title: Beneficiaries Trump Wills
takeaways.description: For most people, retirement accounts and life insurance represent majority of wealth. These pass by beneficiary form, NOT will. Review annually.

takeaways.title: Complexity > Estate Size for Trusts
takeaways.description: Young parents with life insurance need trusts more than wealthy singles. Family situation matters more than asset value.

takeaways.title: Cost of Planning << Cost of No Planning
takeaways.description: Basic planning costs $100-500. Probate costs 3-7% of estate ($3K-$7K per $100K). Conservatorship: $3K-$8K. Family conflict: priceless.
